planting.note: Direct sow on the surface of moist to wet, slightly acidic soil in partial shade to full sun; longleaf lobelia grows naturally in wet, sunny to partially shaded habitats of the Atlantic and Gulf Coastal Plain — wet flatwoods, seepage slopes, and marsh margins.

directions: Plant in moist to wet, slightly acidic soil in partial shade to full sun; longleaf lobelia is a native wetland lobelia of the Gulf and Atlantic Coastal Plain, growing in wet flatwoods, seepage slopes, and the moist to wet margins of ponds and streams. The plant is distinguished by its unusually long, narrow leaves (longleaf) and produces typical two-lipped, bright blue-purple lobelia flowers on tall spikes in late summer and fall — a critical nectar source for ruby-throated hummingbirds during fall migration. Like cardinal flower (L. cardinalis), it belongs to the hummingbird-pollinated blue-purple lobelia guild of wetland habitats. An excellent native for moist to wet, partially shaded garden areas in zone 9a where its combination of height, bold flower spikes, and hummingbird attraction make it a standout plant.

planting.note: Direct sow on the surface of moist to wet, strongly acidic, peaty soil in partial shade to full sun; glade lobelia grows in bogs, seepage slopes, and wet pine flatwoods of the Gulf and Atlantic Coastal Plain.

directions: Plant in moist to wet, strongly acidic, peaty or sandy soil in partial shade to full sun; glade lobelia is a native Gulf Coastal Plain lobelia of wet, acidic, bog-like habitats — seepage bogs, sphagnum seeps, and wet longleaf pine flatwoods — producing beautiful blue to blue-violet flower spikes in late summer through fall. It is named for its occurrence in the wet, open 'glades' (clearings) within longleaf pine flatwoods — the type of habitat that supports many of the most interesting plants of the Gulf South. The blue-violet flowers attract hummingbirds and numerous large native bees. Like other lobelias, it requires consistently moist to wet soil and performs best when never allowed to dry out. An outstanding native for bog gardens, rain gardens, and consistently moist, acidic, partially shaded settings.

planting.note: Direct sow on the surface of wet, sandy, strongly acidic soil in partial shade to full sun; Nuttall's lobelia grows in wet, sandy, acidic depressions, bog edges, and seepage areas of the Atlantic and Gulf Coastal Plain. Seeds are tiny — do not cover.

directions: Sow seeds on the surface of wet, sandy, strongly acidic, organic-rich soil in partial shade to full sun; Nuttall's lobelia is a delicate, small-flowered annual or biennial lobelia of wet, sandy, acidic habitats — pond margins with sandy, bare substrate, seepage slopes, and wet depressions in pine flatwoods. The tiny, pale blue to nearly white flowers are much smaller than those of cardinal flower and great blue lobelia, but produced in airy, branching sprays on thread-thin stems. The plant is adapted to pioneer conditions on bare, wet, sandy, exposed soil — the same disturbed conditions that characterize the margins of seasonally fluctuating ponds and bog pools. It naturalizes in appropriate wet, sandy, acidic garden conditions by self-sowing. A botanically interesting native for bog gardens and naturalistic wetland features in zone 9a.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in spring 4–6 inches deep in moist to wet, well-drained to moderately moist, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; Carolina spider lily grows naturally along stream banks, lake margins, and moist to seasonally flooded woodland openings of the southeastern US.

directions: Plant bulbs in moist to wet, well-drained to moderately moist, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; Carolina spider lily is a native southeastern bulb of moist to wet streambanks and lake margins, producing spectacular clusters of pure white, fragrant flowers in summer with the characteristic long, spidery tepals and central cup ('corona') that gives all Hymenocallis the common name 'spider lily'. The large, strap-like leaves are attractive before, during, and after flowering. The flowers are intensely fragrant, particularly in the evening when they attract large sphinx moths as pollinators. This is one of the most widely grown native spiderlilies for garden use, suitable for rain gardens, pond margins, and wet woodland edges. More cold-hardy than some Hymenocallis species, thriving in zones 6–10.

planting.note: Plant small bulbs 2–3 inches deep in well-drained, dry to moderately moist, slightly alkaline to neutral, clay or loamy soil in full sun; Drummond's rain lily is native to the calcareous prairies and roadsides of Texas and adjacent states, blooming after summer rainfalls.

directions: Plant bulbs in well-drained, dry to moderately moist, slightly alkaline to neutral, clay or loamy soil in full sun; Drummond's rain lily is one of the native white rain lilies of Texas and adjacent states, producing elegant, fragrant, pure white, trumpet-shaped flowers that emerge dramatically within hours to days after summer rainfalls — a phenomenon that inspired the common name. The flowers have a pleasant, sweet fragrance most noticeable in the evening, when they attract sphinx moths. When not in bloom, the narrow, rush-like leaves are inconspicuous. After a summer thunderstorm, a drift of Drummond's rain lily in full bloom is one of the most charming sights of the Texas prairie summer. The bulbs multiply freely to form naturalistic colonies in appropriate dry, calcareous prairie soils. An outstanding, low-water native bulb for the zone 9a garden that naturalizes with minimal care.
